skill.name: react-native-architecture
skill.description: Plan and implement production React Native apps with Expo, routing, native integrations, offline behavior, performance patterns, and mobile release workflows.
skill.summary: Architect Expo and React Native apps with practical production patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported a critical heuristic because markdown examples combine CLI commands, documentation links, and a placeholder service account path. Context review found no executable skill code, no prompt injection, and no credential exfiltration; the remaining concern is that users may copy command examples from documentation.

low_findings.title: Documented CLI Commands Require User Review
low_findings.description: The playbook includes npx, expo, and eas command examples for project setup, builds, submissions, and updates. They are markdown instructions rather than automatically executed code, so the risk is limited to users running commands without review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible inside fenced markdown examples and are not invoked by the skill itself. Confidence is high that this is documentation, but users could still copy and run the commands.

low_findings.title: Credential File Alert Is a Placeholder Path
low_findings.description: The EAS submit example references serviceAccountKeyPath with a placeholder google-services.json path. No credential contents are present, and the line is part of a configuration example.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line contains only a relative file path inside an example configuration. I found no embedded private key, token, or secret value in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: External URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Expo, Expo Router, React Native performance documentation, and FlashList documentation. They do not receive data from the skill and do not indicate exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The links are plain resource references at the end of the playbook. There is no network request code or data transfer tied to these URLs.

low_findings.title: Ruby Backtick Execution Matches Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported backtick locations are markdown fences and inline resource references, not Ruby shell execution. No evidence found of code that executes shell strings through backticks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples are rendered documentation blocks or inline file names. The skill contains markdown guidance, not a Ruby runtime or executable source file.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance and Weak Crypto Alerts Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance and weak cryptography alerts map to React Native routing, platform checks, and descriptive text. No evidence found of host reconnaissance, cryptographic implementation, or malicious probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are ordinary mobile application examples and prose. Their context does not include cryptographic functions or commands that inspect the host system.

dangerous_patterns.title: Composite Heuristic Not Confirmed
dangerous_patterns.description: The static critical heuristic combined command examples, public documentation links, and a placeholder credential path. Manual review did not confirm malicious intent, hidden execution, credential access, or data exfiltration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky elements are present only as documentation examples. The files do not contain executable glue that combines command execution, network access, and credential reads.

content.value_statement: React Native projects often fail when navigation, native APIs, offline data, and releases are planned separately. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a production-oriented playbook for cohesive Expo app architecture.
actual_capabilities: Recommends Expo and bare React Native project structures for mobile apps.
Shows navigation patterns with Expo Router, tabs, stacks, and dynamic routes.
Explains authentication flows using secure storage and protected route groups.
Provides offline-first patterns using React Query persistence and connectivity state.
Covers native integrations such as haptics, biometrics, notifications, and platform-specific UI.
Outlines EAS build, submit, and update workflows for mobile releases.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a complete generated application.
Examples require adaptation to the target product, backend, and compliance needs.
Native module choices may need validation against the current Expo SDK version.
It does not replace testing on physical iOS and Android devices.

use_cases.title: Start a New Expo App
use_cases.description: Define folder structure, routing, state management, secure storage, and release profiles before building the first feature.

use_cases.title: Stabilize an Existing React Native App
use_cases.description: Review navigation, offline data, native module usage, and performance patterns to reduce crashes and slow screens.

use_cases.title: Plan Mobile Release Workflows
use_cases.description: Create a practical EAS build, submit, and OTA update plan for development, preview, and production channels.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Starter Architecture
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new app
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the React Native architecture skill to propose an Expo app structure for my product. Include navigation groups, state management, secure storage, and release profiles.

prompt_templates.title: Design Navigation and Auth
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a React Native navigation and authentication flow. Include protected routes, login redirects, tab screens, and deep link considerations.

prompt_templates.title: Add Offline-First Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the skill to design offline-first data behavior for my React Native app. Cover caching, optimistic updates, connectivity changes, and conflict handling.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill as a production readiness checklist for my React Native app. Review native integrations, performance, platform differences, EAS builds, and release risks.

output_examples.input: Plan the architecture for a marketplace app built with Expo.
output_examples.output: A structured architecture plan covering route groups, shared components, API services, offline cache strategy, secure token storage, notifications, and EAS release profiles.

output_examples.input: Review why my React Native list screen feels slow.
output_examples.output: A focused performance review that recommends virtualized list settings, memoized row components, stable callbacks, image optimization, and device testing steps.

output_examples.input: Prepare an app for production submission.
output_examples.output: A release checklist for development, preview, and production builds, including store submission setup, OTA update policy, platform testing, and rollback planning.

best_practices: Validate each suggested package against the Expo SDK and React Native version in the project.
Test native features on real iOS and Android devices before release.
Keep secrets out of source code and use platform-approved secure storage for tokens.
anti_patterns: Do not copy build or submit commands into production automation without reviewing accounts and profiles.
Do not store authentication tokens in AsyncStorage when secure storage is available.
Do not treat simulator behavior as proof that native integrations work on devices.

faq.question: Does this skill create a full React Native app?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture guidance, patterns, examples, and checklists that must be adapted to your app.

faq.question: Can it help with Expo Router?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes route groups, tab navigation, stack navigation, dynamic routes, and programmatic navigation patterns.

faq.question: Does it support offline-first apps?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains React Query persistence, connectivity state, stale data handling, and optimistic updates.

faq.question: Can it guide native module integration?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers examples for haptics, biometrics, notifications, and platform-specific components.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with low risk. The audit found documentation examples, not hidden execution or credential exfiltration.

faq.question: What should users review before running examples?
faq.answer: Users should review package versions, EAS account settings, store credentials, and the target release profile.
